planting.note: Allow cuttings to dry and callus for 1–2 weeks before planting in extremely well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; do not water until new growth appears — plumeria cuttings rot easily if kept wet before rooting. Established plants are dormant and leafless in winter and require complete dryness during dormancy.

directions: Plant cuttings in extremely well-drained, sandy soil in full sun — plumeria is a tropical American tree sacred in Buddhist and Hindu cultures, widely planted around temples throughout Asia and used in Hawaiian leis, and one of the most intensely fragrant of all flowering plants. The waxy, 4-inch flowers in white, yellow, pink, red, or multicolor combinations have a powerful sweet tropical scent that is strongest at night. Plumeria is deciduous (leafless in winter), drought tolerant, and thrives in heat — full sun and excellent drainage are its only requirements. In zone 9a it is widely grown in containers: bring indoors dry and leafless when temperatures drop below 40°F, store in a frost-free location, and return outdoors in spring. Hundreds of named cultivars are prized by collectors; the Singapore White (P. obtusa) is an evergreen species suited to zone 10+.

planting.note: Nick or scarify the hard seed coat and soak 24 hours before planting, or plant rooted container specimens in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; golden shower tree is fast-growing and drought tolerant once established. In zone 9a, plant against a south-facing wall for frost protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; golden shower tree is one of the most spectacular of all tropical flowering trees — the national tree of Thailand — producing cascading chains of brilliant golden-yellow flowers 12–18 inches long that completely cover the tree before the new leaves emerge in spring, creating a breathtaking golden cascade. It grows fast (3–5 feet per year) in frost-free conditions, reaching 25–40 feet, and tolerates drought, alkaline soil, and seaside conditions once established. The flowers are edible in some cultures (flower buds in curries). In zone 9a it may die back in severe freezes but typically regrows from established root systems; site in a protected location sheltered from north winds. A magnificent specimen or street tree for zone 9–11 Gulf Coast landscapes.

planting.note: Plant tubers 2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after soil warms to 60°F; tuberose requires a long, warm growing season to bloom — plant early and do not allow soil to remain cold and wet, which causes rot. In zone 9–11 tubers can remain in the ground year-round with light mulch.

directions: Plant tubers 2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; tuberose is one of the most intensely fragrant flowers in the world — the pure white, waxy flowers on tall spikes in late summer have a heavy, sweet, complex scent that intensifies at night and has been prized for centuries in perfumery and garlands. It is the principal flower in traditional South Asian garlands and is widely used in the Indian perfume industry (the absolute is one of the most expensive floral extracts). The single-flowered variety blooms slightly earlier than the double; 'The Pearl' is the most widely grown double-flowered cultivar. In zone 9–11 the clumping tubers naturalize to form large colonies that bloom reliably each summer; in colder zones dig and store tubers dry through winter. An outstanding cut flower with exceptional vase life of 10–14 days.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade in October–November; unlike most daffodils, paperwhites require no cold chilling period and bloom naturally in the warmth of the Gulf South winter, typically December–February. In zone 9–10 they naturalize and multiply freely.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade in fall; paperwhites are the most reliable and easy-to-grow winter-blooming bulb for the Gulf South, where they naturalize freely and bloom reliably every December–February without any cold treatment. The clusters of small, pure white, extremely fragrant flowers are a joy in the winter garden and make excellent cut flowers — the strong musky-sweet scent is distinctive and potent (dilute in mixed arrangements for those sensitive to strong fragrances). Unlike yellow daffodils, paperwhites persist and multiply in the warm, mild winters of zone 8–11. Plant new bulbs in fall for the first year; established naturalized clumps increase every year and require only to be divided when crowded. Leave the foliage to die back naturally after blooming to feed the bulbs for next year.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions horizontally in a wide, shallow container of heavy clay soil without drainage holes, then submerge 12–24 inches below the water surface in full sun; water lily requires at least 6 hours of direct sun daily for good flowering — too much shade dramatically reduces bloom production.

directions: Plant rhizomes in heavy clay soil in a wide, shallow container submerged 12–24 inches below the water surface in full sun; the fragrant water lily is the quintessential aquatic ornamental — its floating, circular pads 6–12 inches across and beautiful white or pink flowers 3–5 inches wide that open each morning and close by noon are iconic in ponds and water gardens across North America. N. odorata is native to eastern North America and is the most fragrant of the hardy water lilies, perfuming the air around the pond with a sweet, sweet scent. The rhizomes are edible (a traditional Native American food) and the seeds and flower buds were also consumed. Divide rhizomes every 2–3 years when they crowd their container; feed during the growing season with aquatic plant tablets pushed into the soil. In zone 3–7 lower the container to the pond bottom below the freeze line for winter protection.

common_name: Curcuma (Siam Tulip, Hidden Lily, Thai Tulip)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; curcumas emerge from dormancy late (May–June), so patience is required. In zone 8–9 mulch heavily in fall and the rhizomes typically overwinter successfully in the ground.

directions: Plant rhizomes 2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Curcuma alismatifolia (Siam tulip) is one of the most striking ornamental gingers, producing elegant upright flower spikes in late summer with a cone of showy bracts in pink, purple, or white, resembling a large tulip — hence the name. The broad, tropical foliage and exotic inflorescences make curcumas outstanding cut flowers and exceptional accent plants in the shaded summer border. They emerge late in spring from dormancy (don't panic and dig them — wait until June), grow and flower through summer, then die back to the ground in fall. The ornamental Curcumas differ from culinary turmeric (C. longa) but the two are closely related; several curcuma species have both edible and ornamental value.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; costus is one of the most shade-tolerant of all ornamental gingers and grows naturally as a forest understory plant in tropical Asia. In zone 8–9 the tops die back in frost but roots resprout reliably from established clumps.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; spiral ginger is one of the most structurally unusual and striking plants for the tropical shade garden — the broad, elliptical leaves are arranged in a tight spiral up the stem (giving it the name 'stepladder plant'), and the plant culminates in a dense, pinecone-like terminal inflorescence of overlapping red bracts from which large, white, orchid-like flowers emerge one at a time over several weeks. The architectural form of the spiraling stems creates a dramatic, bold presence in the shaded garden. It is one of the best shade-tolerant tropicals for Gulf Coast gardens and makes an excellent companion for heliconias, anthuriums, and alocasias in the tropical shade garden. Cut old stems to the ground after flowering; new stems arise from the base.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, chunky, humus-rich, acidic epiphytic mix (orchid bark, perlite, and peat) in bright indirect light or deep shade; anthurium roots require excellent air circulation and will rot in dense, waterlogged soil. Keep roots slightly elevated if possible for best drainage.

directions: Plant in chunky, well-drained epiphytic mix in bright indirect light to deep shade — anthurium produces some of the most striking and long-lasting of all tropical flowers, with waxy, heart-shaped spathes in red, pink, white, coral, or burgundy surrounding a contrasting spadix, each spathe remaining attractive for 2–3 months. It is one of the most popular tropical houseplants worldwide and the most important cut flower in Hawaii's floriculture industry. As a garden plant in zone 10–12, it thrives in the deep shade of trees and covered patios, exactly where little else grows. The glossy, dark green leaves are themselves ornamental. It is sensitive to direct sun (which burns the leaves), overwatering, and temperatures below 55°F. In zone 9a it is an outstanding container plant for shaded patios, wintering indoors. Keep soil moderately moist and fertilize lightly every 6 weeks.

planting.note: Plant in virtually any soil type — including poor, rocky, saline, or waterlogged soils — in full sun; noni is one of the most adaptable and stress-tolerant of all tropical trees and will grow in conditions that defeat most other fruit trees. It is sensitive to frost.

directions: Plant in virtually any soil — sandy, clay, rocky, or saline — in full sun; noni is a remarkable tropical tree of extraordinary stress tolerance, the sacred plant of Polynesian cultures for thousands of years, propagated across the Pacific by canoe voyagers. The large, shiny, deeply veined leaves and lumpy, white, pungent fruits borne continuously throughout the year make it an unusual and productive ornamental. The ripe fruit has a very strong fermented cheese-like smell that limits its appeal for eating fresh — it is typically consumed as juice, dried powder, or fermented preparations widely marketed as a health supplement with traditional anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties (scientifically studied but not conclusively proven). Noni is one of the very few tropical fruit trees that grows and fruits in pure beach sand with salt spray exposure, making it valuable in coastal zone 10+ landscapes.

planting.note: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; ylang-ylang grows very rapidly (6–15 feet per year when young) and produces the characteristic drooping, chandelier-like crown. It is frost sensitive and suited to outdoor culture in zone 10–12 only.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; ylang-ylang is the source of one of the most important perfume ingredients in the world — the intensely fragrant yellow-green flowers with long, drooping petals are steam-distilled to produce the essential oil used in Chanel No. 5, Joy, and countless other perfumes. The scent is rich, heavy, and intensely sweet with notes of jasmine, narcissus, and banana. The tree grows remarkably fast — producing a graceful, weeping tropical specimen in just a few years — with pendulous branches and large, oval leaves. Flowers are produced year-round in tropical climates and are used fresh in garlands and as bedroom perfumers throughout Southeast Asia and the Pacific. Ylang-ylang is the national flower of the Philippines, where it is called 'poor man's perfume.' Only suited to outdoor culture in frost-free zone 10–12.
